# Resources and resource types - Windmill
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-11, [Read more](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/core_concepts/resources_and_types)

Resources are rich objects in JSON that allow to store configuration and credentials.
In Windmill, Resources represent connections to third-party systems.
Resources are a good way to define a connection to a frequently used third-party system such as a database.
Think of Resources as a structured way to store configuration and credentials, and access them from scripts.
...
This is how Windmill connects to any third-party system: pick one of the 200+ resource types available on the Hub, or define your own for anything internal or not yet covered.
...
) or resources (
```
res://path/to/resource
```
...
To create a resource using an existing type, go to the Resources page and click "Add resource".
...
Windmill comes preloaded with some common Resource types, such as databases, apps, SMTP, etc. You can see the full list on Windmill Hub. You can also add custom Resource types by clicking "Add a resource type" on the Resources page.

# Add resources and variables to code | Windmill
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-11, [Read more](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/code_editor/add_variables_resources)

You can directly access Variables and Resources from the Code editor.
...
Provided you have the right permissions and the resource type exists in the workspace, you can access resource types from scripts, flows and apps using the Windmill client or TypedDict in Python.
From the code editor's toolbar, click on the
```
+ Type
```
button and pick the right resource type.
...
You can also edit the Resource or even create a new one right from the Code editor.
### Fetching them from within a script by using the wmill client in the respective language​
By clicking on
```
+ Resource
```
, you'll get to pick a resource from your workspace and be able to fetch it from within the script.
TypeScript:
```
wmill.getResource('u/user/foo');
```
Python:
```
wmill.get_resource("u/user/foo")
```
Go:
```
wmill.GetResource("u/user/foo")
```
Bash:
```
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $WM_TOKEN" \
"$BASE_INTERNAL_URL/api/w/$WM_WORKSPACE/resources/get/u/user/foo" \
| jq -r .value
```

# Windmill MCP
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-08, [Read more](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/core_concepts/mcp)

- **Resources**- Create, read, update, and delete resources for third-party system connections like databases and APIs
...
MCP exposes Windmill's resource management as endpoint tools.
You can create or inspect resources conversationally:
> "List all my PostgreSQL resources in Windmill"
> "Create a new Slack resource called 'alerts-channel' with webhook URL https://hooks.slack.com/services/T00/B00/xxx"
Claude will use the **Resources** endpoint tools to list, create, or update resources in your workspace without leaving the chat.
...
### Resources & Variables​
|Tool|Description|
|--|--|
|``` listResource ```|List all resources|
|``` getResource ```|Get a resource|
|``` createResource ```|Create a new resource|
|``` updateResource ```|Update an existing resource|
|``` deleteResource ```|Delete a resource|
|``` listResourceType ```|List available resource types|
|``` listVariable ```|List all variables|

part.text:
**Exploring AI Configurations**

I'm currently focused on understanding the AI resource allocation. My recent thought is about the apparent contradiction: if no AI resources are in the workspace, how does it leverage an AI provider? This leads me to examine the instance-level configuration for AI providers, models, and prompts.

**Investigating Provider Configuration**

I'm digging into how AI providers are utilized. It appears the instance-level AI configuration is indeed inherited. My current focus is on understanding how an AI Agent step consumes this global setting. Specifically, I'm exploring whether omitting the provider from input transforms, or setting it to null, triggers an error, as the system seems to expect it.

input.content:
summary: AI Agent with a Structured-Output Tool
description: "AI Agent that can call a SKU lookup tool and return structured price information."
value:
  modules:
    - id: price_agent_step
      summary: AI Agent Step
      value:
        type: aiagent
        parallel: false
        tools:
          - id: lookup_sku
            summary: Look up unit price and currency for a SKU
            value:
              tool_type: flowmodule
              type: script
              path: f/agent_zr_fldjzzb/lookup_sku
        input_transforms:
          provider:
            type: static
            value: null
          user_message:
            type: javascript
            expr: flow_input.prompt
          output_schema:
            type: static
            value:
              type: object
              properties:
                sku:
                  type: string
                unit_price:
                  type: number
                currency:
                  type: string
              required:
                - sku
                - unit_price
                - currency
has_on_behalf_of: false
schema:
  $schema: https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema
  type: object
  order:
    - prompt
  properties:
    prompt:
      type: string
      description: The prompt for the AI Agent
  required:
    - prompt

input.command: cd /home/user/wm-agent && wmill flow preview f/agent_zr_fldjzzb/price_agent__flow --workspace default -d '{"prompt": "Look up the unit price for SKU WIDGET-42 and reply with the structured answer."}'

I've identified a `serde_json` error indicating a null value where a `ProviderWithResource` struct was expected. I'm now examining the expected fields of this struct to understand the source of this discrepancy.

**Clarifying Configuration Fields**

I've realized `ProviderWithResource` maps to `ProviderConfig`. I'm now reviewing `ProviderConfig`'s schema, specifically its `resource` field, to determine if setting it to `null`, an empty string, or a default path might resolve the `serde_json` error.

# Resources and resource types
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-11, [Read more](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/core_concepts/resources_and_types)

Each Resource has a **Resource Type** (RT for short) - for example MySQL, MongoDB, OpenAI, etc.
- that defines the schema that the resource of this type needs to implement.
Schemas implement the JSON Schema specification.
This is how Windmill connects to any third-party system: pick one of the 200+ resource types available on the Hub, or define your own for anything internal or not yet covered.
There are no vendor-branded connectors - the resource type is the integration.
...
Windmill comes preloaded with some common Resource types, such as databases, apps, SMTP, etc. You can see the full list on Windmill Hub. You can also add custom Resource types by clicking "Add a resource type" on the Resources page.

# Resources - Windmill
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-04-07, [Read more](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/advanced/cli/resource)

The
```
wmill resource
```
list command is used to list all resources in the remote workspace.
```
wmill resource
```
...
The
```
wmill resource push
```
command is used to push a local resource, overriding any existing remote versions.
```
wmill resource push <file_path> <remote_path>
```
...
|``` file_path ```|The path to the resource file to push.|
...
### Resource specification​
We support both JSON and YAML files.
The structure of the file is as follows:
```
value: <value>
description: <description>
resource_type: <resource_type>
is_oauth: <is_oauth>
```
```
"value": "<value>",
"description": "<description>",
"resource_type": "<resource_type>",
"is_oauth": "<is_oauth>"
```
- value (required): Represents the actual content or value of the resource.
- description (optional): A string providing additional information or a description of the resource.
- resource_type (required): A resource type
- is_oauth (optional, deprecated): This property is deprecated and should not be used.

# Windmill Guide - GitHub Gist
created: 2025-12-23, last updated: 2026-05-21, [Read more](https://gist.github.com/tomdavidson/93157c2faf91561d8f2263bb28634f76)

### Schema Definition
```
schema:
type: object
properties:
email:
type: string
format: email
description: "User email"
count:
type: integer
minimum: 1
maximum: 100
database:
type: object
format: "resource-postgresql"
required: ["email"]
order: ["email", "count", "database"]
...
- The app is fully declarative:
- Components: `id`, `type`, `configuration`, `position`.
- Runnables: connections to scripts/flows.
- Bindings: expressions wiring component outputs to runnables and other components.
- Policy: execution identity and allowed runnables.

# Integrations on Windmill
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-23, [Read more](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/integrations/integrations_on_windmill)

Using Windmill, integrations are referred to as resources and resource types.
Each Resource has a Resource Type (RT for short) - for example MySQL, MongoDB, Slack, etc.
- that defines the schema that the resource needs to implement.
...
- A resource type defines the JSON schema (host, credentials, options) for that system.
Pick one of the 200+ resource types on the Hub, or define your own.
- A resource holds an instance of that schema, with variables and secrets inlined for credentials.
- Scripts in any supported language receive the resource as a typed parameter and call the system using whatever client library or CLI is appropriate.
...
Resources are structured configurations and connections to third-party systems, with Resource types defining the schema for each Resource.
